# B01: Detailed Recap

## Meeting Overview
V (Vrijen Attawar) met with Chris Hanson, founder of 360 — an edtech product focused on executive function coaching for individuals with ADHD, particularly in K-12 settings. The conversation began as a personal connection through their shared executive function coach (Heather from Chris's organization) and evolved into a substantive product strategy and go-to-market advisory session. V provided candid startup guidance drawn from Careerspan's parallel journey as a non-technical founder in edtech.

## Chronological Discussion

### Personal Connection & Coaching Context (0:00–3:00)
The meeting opened casually, with V mentioning his work with Heather (an executive function coach at Chris's organization). Chris probed whether V's self-deprecating language around "bad habits" had shifted through coaching — a revealing moment that showed Chris's practitioner instincts. V offered a thoughtful reflection on the duality of viewing behaviors neutrally versus falling into self-judgment, and both connected over the ADHD experience of balancing novelty-seeking with the need for consistency. V noted he was glad to be having this conversation, having been giving Heather a tour of his AI systems when the introduction happened.

### V's AI Interest & Chris's Builder Journey (3:00–6:00)
V shared his long-standing interest in building AI-powered "brain crutches" for ADHD — tools that fill executive function gaps beyond therapy, medication, and coaching. Chris revealed he's been building his product for almost a year, coming from a background as a special education teacher turned small business owner with no technical background. V gave genuine recognition of the difficulty of what Chris accomplished — noting that the product website looked well-assembled for someone outside the tech world. Chris credited Joe Coleman as his technical partner, describing him as a "jack of all trades" handyman-style developer.

### Non-Technical Founder Solidarity & Technical Navigation (6:00–8:15)
V drew direct parallels to his own journey at Careerspan — non-technical, came from management consulting, faced painful early experiences trusting the wrong technical people. He emphasized that navigating the technical world without a dedicated technical advisor is exceptionally difficult, and gave Chris credit for articulating product requirements to Joe without a product manager intermediary. Chris acknowledged he only recently realized a PM layer would have helped, but noted the constraints of being bootstrapped with no investors.

### Monetization Strategy & Go-to-Market (8:15–13:30)
Chris outlined his monetization plan: **B2B/B2G sales to organizations, school districts, clinics, and supportive organizations**. Current pricing: $10/month individual, $20/month for supporters (up to 10 profiles), plus a "request a quote" enterprise option. He expressed concern about organizations demanding extensive feature development as a condition of purchase.

V delivered pointed strategic advice:
- **Don't over-optimize for one stakeholder** — the risk of building custom features for a single buyer at the expense of the broader product
- **Avoid the "pilot death spiral"** — Careerspan's own experience of doing pilot after pilot with incremental feature additions
- **Seek a paid design partner** — find someone willing to put money down (even modest amounts) and work with the product as-is, rather than demanding a wish list
- **A cornerstone customer creates options** — reliable revenue base enables either expansion, new customer acquisition, or fundraising leverage

### Sales Process & ICP Refinement (13:30–18:45)
V strongly advised against pursuing venture capital, drawing on personal experience: *"They're not worth your time, my friend."* Instead, he recommended:
- **Hire an SDR for lead generation** while Chris continues closing deals personally (doubles as customer research, network building, and messaging refinement)
- **Run a focused push of customer interviews** to narrow the ICP further
- Shared Careerspan's hard-won lesson: *"Every time I thought we were being too narrow, we were still being too broad"*
- Explained product-market fit through two memorable framings: the Supreme Court's definition of obscenity ("you know it when you see it") and the anxiety test ("when you have it, you don't think about it; when you don't, you think about it all the time")

### Community Building & Authenticity (19:00–25:00)
Chris raised the idea of a Facebook group for users and trial users. V mapped out a **community engagement spectrum**: one-to-many broadcasting → social media with comments → private sub-community for top users → full community. He cautioned Chris to be extremely efficient with time given it's just him and Joe.

A notable exchange on authenticity: Chris explained his reluctance around social media — he naturally curses, but serves kids and parents who might not appreciate that from their child's coach. V suggested **separating personas across different channels** for different audiences, and recommended Chris apply to **FOHE (Future of Higher Education)**, the largest higher ed Slack community globally (~6,000 members), where V serves as New York co-lead. V offered to make introductions once Chris locks down a linchpin school deal.

### Personal Details & Relationship Building (25:00–28:30)
Chris lives in Kent, Washington (between Seattle and Tacoma); V is in Brooklyn. V shared that his wife originally found Chris's organization when looking into executive function coaching for him. He'd initially spoken with Amy (client onboarding specialist) before being matched with Heather. Both founders bonded over the rewards of entrepreneurship — hiring people you like, working authentically, and the stress-reward tradeoff of being your own boss.

## Key Takeaways
- **Chris Hanson runs a bootstrapped edtech startup (360)** focused on executive function coaching tools, primarily targeting K-12 school districts and organizations — approximately one year into development with a non-technical founder profile very similar to V's
- **Massive distribution asset**: 65,000 active email subscribers, which V identified as a significant competitive advantage for go-to-market
- **V provided direct strategic counsel**: avoid VC, find a paid design partner/cornerstone customer, hire an SDR, run customer interviews to narrow ICP, and beware the pilot death spiral
- **FOHE introduction offered**: V invited Chris to apply to the Future of Higher Education Slack community (~6,000 members) with V as reference and New York co-lead, plus offered to make intros once Chris has a linchpin deal
- **Strong personal rapport**: shared ADHD experience, non-technical founder solidarity, and mutual connection through Heather's coaching created authentic chemistry
- **Chris's key challenge is the transition from product to repeatable sales** — he has the domain expertise, the audience, and a functional product, but needs structured go-to-market execution and ICP discipline
